name: cissp-content-system
description: "Model CISSP curriculum depth, domain breakdowns, exercises, mastery tracking, spaced repetition, adaptive review, and localization-ready content pipelines. Use for deep dives by domain and future certification content systems."
---

# CISSP Content System

## When to Use
- Designing the curriculum hierarchy
- Planning content storage and metadata
- Creating question banks, drills, flashcards, and scenario exercises
- Defining adaptive review and mastery logic

## CISSP Domain Baseline
1. Security and Risk Management
2. Asset Security
3. Security Architecture and Engineering
4. Communication and Network Security
5. Identity and Access Management
6. Security Assessment and Testing
7. Security Operations
8. Software Development Security

Version the outline so the platform can track updates from the certification body without breaking learner progress.

## Recommended Content Hierarchy
- Program
- Domain
- Module
- Lesson
- Concept block
- Worked example
- Exercise set
- Review queue item

## Exercise Types
- Concept recall
- Scenario-based multiple choice
- Short explanation prompts
- Compare-and-contrast drills
- Incident or architecture case studies
- Confidence rating after each answer

## Feedback Rules
- Every exercise needs rationale, not just correctness.
- Explain why the right answer is right and why the distractors are wrong.
- Track misconception tags so review plans can target weak concepts.
- Keep difficulty progressive and explicit.

## Mastery Model
- Capture correctness, confidence, response time, and retry count.
- Promote concepts from exposure to practice to mastery.
- Use spaced repetition for weak or aging concepts.
- Distinguish exam readiness from long-term retention.

## Localization Rules
- Keep canonical source content separated from translations.
- Version translations independently from learner attempts.
- Store glossary terms so security vocabulary stays consistent across languages.

## Future Expansion Rules
- Keep certification-specific taxonomy in metadata, not hard-coded tables.
- Reuse the same content engine for other training programs.
- Support multiple exercise formats without changing the core mastery model.
